figures.caption: FIG. 4: (Color online) Energy spectrum of a d-wave altramagnet with dx2−y2 pairing symmetry in the presence of the spinorbit coupling (ρ = 0.53) and an off-resonance dressing field with effective coupling parameter Kω = 0.6 k(0) and β = 0.9 (nearly circular polarization). Panels (a) and (b) demonstrate the spin-resolved (s = ±1) anisotropic energy dispersions ϵs(k⃗) obtained from Eq. (6) as the functions of the kx- and ky- components of the wave vector k⃗. The black and red lines correspond to the values of spin index s = ±1. Plots (c) and (d) represent the constant-energy cuts corresponding to ϵ1 = 2.0E(0) and ϵ1 = 1.0E(0). Here, the red and black curves are related to the positive and negative out-of-plane spin polarizations

FIG. 10: (Color online) Energy spectrum of a d-wave altramagnet with dx2−y2 pairing symmetry in the presence of the spinorbit coupling (ρ = 0.53) and an off-resonance linearly polarized (β = 0) dressing field with effective coupling parameter Kω = 0.6 k(0). The direction of the linear polarization of the imposed radiation is along the y−axis (θ0 = π/2). Panels (a) and (b) demonstrate the spin-resolved (s = ±1) anisotropic energy dispersions ϵs(k⃗) obtainedfrom Eq. (6) as the functions of the kx- and ky-components of the wave vector k⃗. The black and red lines correspond to the values of spin index s = ±1. Plots (c) and (d) represent the constant-energy cuts corresponding to ϵ1 = 2.0E(0) and ϵ1 = 1.0E(0). Here, the red and black curves are

FIG. 11: (Color online) The transverse components χxy of spin-type Edelstein susceptibilities in the units of e/(4π2) for a d-wave altramagnet with dx2−y2 pairing symmetry in the presence of the spin-orbit coupling and an off-resonance dressing field. The chemical potential corresponds to the energy ϵs(k⃗ = 0) for each of these graphs. The left panels (a), (c) and (e) demonstrate how susceptibility χxy depends On the strength of Rashba spin-orbit coupling for different cases of electron-photon coupling parameter Kω = 0.0 (zero irradiation, black curves), Kω = 0.5 k(0) (Red curves) and Kω = 0.7 k(0) (blue curves). The right panels (b), (d) and (f) how susceptibility χxy depends on the electron-photon coupling parameter Kω for different cases of Rashba spin-orbit coupling ρ = 0.3 (black curves), ρ = 0.4 (red curves) and ρ = 0.5 (blue curves).

FIG. 5: (Color online) Energy spectrum of a d-wave altramagnet with dx2−y2 pairing symmetry in the absence of the spin-orbit coupling (ρ = 0) but exposed to an off-resonance dressing field with effective coupling parameter Kω = 0.6 k(0) and β = 0.9 (nearly circular polarization). Panels (a) and (b) demonstrate the spin-resolved (s = ±1) anisotropic energy dispersions ϵs(k⃗) obtained from Eq. (6) as the functions of the kx- and ky- components of the wave vector k⃗. The black and red lines correspond to the values of spin index s = ±1. Plots (c) and (d) represent the constant-energy cuts corresponding to ϵ1 = 2.0E(0) and ϵ1 = 1.0E(0). Here, the red and black curves are related to the positive and negative out-of-plane spin polarizations

FIG. 9: (Color online) Energy spectrum of a d-wave altramagnet with dx2−y2 pairing symmetry in the presence of the spinorbit coupling (ρ = 0.53) and an off-resonance linearly polarized (β = 0) dressing field with effective coupling parameter Kω = 0.6 k(0). The direction of the linear polarization of the imposed radiation is along the x−axis (θ0 = 0). Panels (a) and (b) demonstrate the spin-resolved (s = ±1) anisotropic energy dispersions ϵs(k⃗) obtained from Eq. (6) as the functions of the kx- and ky-components of the wave vector k⃗. The black and red lines correspond to the values of spin index s = ±1. Plots (c) and (d) represent the constant-energy cuts corresponding to ϵ1 = 2.0E(0) and ϵ1 = 1.0E(0). FIG. 1: (Color online) The schematics for a setup of a two-dimensional altermagnetic material (d-wave type) in the presence of an off-resonance dressing field with different polarizations (generally elliptical; circular, and linear, as limiting cases). The electrostatic gating provided by the two electrodes enables Rashba spin-orbit coupling in this material.

FIG. 6: (Color online) Energy spectrum of a d-wave altramagnet with dx2−y2 pairing symmetry in the presence of the spinorbit coupling (ρ = 0.53) and an off-resonance dressing field with effective coupling parameter Kω = 0.6 k(0) and β = 0.2 (close to the linear polarization). Panels (a) and (b) demonstrate the spin-resolved (s = ±1) anisotropic energy dispersions ϵs(k⃗) obtained from Eq. (6) as the functions of the kx- and ky-components of the wave vector k⃗. The black and red lines correspond to the values of spin index s = ±1. Plots (c) and (d) represent the constant-energy cuts corresponding to ϵ1 = 2.0E(0) and ϵ1 = 1.0E(0). Here, the red and black curves are related to the positive and negative out-of-plane spin polarizations

FIG. 2: (Color online). Energy spectrum of a d-wave altramagnet with dx2−y2 pairing symmetry in the absence of the spin-orbit coupling (ρ = 0). Panels (a) and (b) demonstrate the spin-resolved (s = ±1) anisotropic energy dispersions ϵs(k⃗) obtained from Eq. (6) as the functions of the kx- and ky- components of the wave vector k⃗. The black and red lines correspond to the values of spin index s = ±1. Plots (c) and (d) represent the constant-energy cuts corresponding to ϵ1 = 2.0E(0) and ϵ1 = 1.0E(0). Here, the red and black curves are related to the positive and negative out-of-plane spin polarizations

FIG. 8: (Color online) The energy bandgap ∆0(ρ, β | Kω, ω) between the two spin-resolved bands (s = ±1) in a d-wave altermagnet with dx2−y2 pairing symmetry in the presence of the spin-orbit coupling and an off-resonance dressing field of different polarizations. Panel (a) demonstrates a contour plot of the bandgap ∆0 as a function of ratio of field strengths along the two axes of the polarization ellipse beta and effective coupling parameter Kω. Plot (b) represents the bandgap ∆0 as a function of parameter data and the strength of the spin orbit coupling ρ = rSO/k(0), as labeled.

FIG. 7: (Color online) Energy spectrum of a d-wave altramagnet with dxy pairing symmetry in the presence of the spin-orbit coupling (ρ = 0.53) and an off-resonance dressing field with effective coupling parameter Kω = 0.6 k(0) and β = 0.9 (nearly circular polarization). Panels (a) and (b) demonstrate the spin-resolved (s = ±1) anisotropic energy dispersions ϵs(k⃗) obtained from Eq. (6) as the functions of the kx- and ky- components of the wave vector k⃗. The black and red lines correspond to the values of spin index s = ±1. Plots (c) and (d) represent the constant-energy cuts corresponding to ϵ1 = 2.0E(0) and ϵ1 = 1.0E(0). Here, the red and black curves are related to the positive and negative out-of-plane spin polarizations

FIG. 12: (Color online) The transverse components χxy of spin-type Edelstein susceptibilities in the units of e/(4π2) for a dwave altermagnet with dxy pairing symmetry in the presence of the spin-orbit coupling and an off-resonance dressing field. The chemical potential corresponds to the energy ϵs(k⃗ = 0) for each of these graphs. The left panels (a), (c) and (e) demonstrate how susceptibility χxy depends On the strength of Rashba spin-orbit coupling for different cases of electron-photon coupling parameter Kω = 0.0 (zero irradiation, black curves), Kω = 0.5 k(0) (Red curves) and Kω = 0.7 k(0) (blue curves). The right panels (b), (d) and (f) how susceptibility χxy depends on the electron-photon coupling parameter Kω for different cases of Rashba spin-orbit coupling ρ = 0.3 (black curves), ρ = 0.4 (red curves) and ρ = 0.5 (blue curves).
